repository_objective: Generate, test, deploy, and validate IaC templates for full-stack application environments on GCP.

project_context.note: Always deploy to us-central1. Do not spread resources across multiple regions unless the template explicitly requires multi-region (e.g., global load balancers, multi-region buckets). Use the project_id above — never prompt the user or leave PROJECT_ID as a placeholder.

apis_enabled.guidance: If a template requires an API not in already_enabled, add a google_project_service resource to the Terraform (or enable it via gcloud before applying KCC). Do not assume APIs are enabled.

networking.strategy: Create a dedicated VPC per template. Do NOT reuse forge-network for GKE clusters — it has no secondary IP ranges and its /24 subnet (10.0.0.0/24) cannot fit the pod and service CIDRs required for VPC-native GKE.

per_template_vpc_pattern.description: Allocate CIDRs in increments of 16 through the 10.0.0.0/8 space. Check existing templates to avoid conflicts.

networking.naming: Name VPCs and subnets after the template: <template-name>-vpc, <template-name>-subnet
networking.cleanup: Terraform destroy will remove the per-template VPC. No shared network state to worry about.
networking.private_google_access: Always enable private_ip_google_access = true on subnets so private clusters can reach Google APIs.

machine_types.guidance: Match machine type to workload. Use spot/preemptible for all sandbox validation to cut costs. For scarce accelerators (A2, v6e TPUs), use DWS + Kueue — see accelerator_strategy below.

recommendations.use_case: GPU inference (L4 — preferred for LLM serving)

accelerator_strategy.description: For templates requiring scarce accelerators (A2/A100, v6e TPUs, future H100s), use Dynamic Workload Scheduler (DWS) with Kueue rather than requesting nodes immediately. DWS queues the workload and provisions capacity when available — validation may take hours or up to a week for highly contested hardware.
when_to_use_dws_kueue: Any template using A2 (A100 40GB) node pools
Any template using TPU v5e, v6e slice node pools
Any template where the target accelerator has <50% quota headroom in the region

dws_kueue_pattern.node_pool_config: Set queued_provisioning { enabled = true } on the node pool in Terraform. This enables DWS.
dws_kueue_pattern.kueue_install: Install Kueue via Helm: helm install kueue oci://us-docker.pkg.dev/gke-release-packages/helm-charts/kueue --version <latest>
required_resources: ClusterQueue — defines the resource budget (GPU/TPU quotas)
LocalQueue — namespace-scoped queue that workloads submit to
ResourceFlavor — maps to the physical node pool
dws_kueue_pattern.workload_annotation: Jobs/Pods must have kueue.x-k8s.io/queue-name: <local-queue-name> label to be managed by Kueue

accelerator_strategy.validation_timeout_guidance: When a template uses DWS/Kueue, set the CI validation timeout to 7 days (10080 minutes) rather than the default 10 minutes. The workflow will poll for completion. Document the expected wait time in verification_plan.md.
accelerator_strategy.pre_check_script: Before deploying any accelerator template, run the quota and availability pre-check (see pre_deployment_checks below).

pre_deployment_checks.description: Before applying any template that requests accelerators or significant compute, run these checks and abort (with an issue comment) if they fail. This prevents wasted deployment attempts against unavailable capacity.

quota_check.command: gcloud compute regions describe us-central1 --project=gca-gke-2025 --format=json | python3 -c "import json,sys; r=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(q['metric'], q['usage'], '/', q['limit']) for q in r['quotas'] if q['limit'] > 0 and q['usage']/q['limit'] > 0.8]"
quota_check.rule: If any required quota metric is above 80% utilisation, post a warning comment on the issue and consider a smaller machine type or a different zone. Abort if above 95%.

accelerator_availability_check.command: gcloud compute machine-types list --filter='zone:us-central1 AND name:<machine-type>' --format='table(name,zone,deprecated.state)' 2>/dev/null
accelerator_availability_check.stock_check: gcloud beta compute future-reservations list --project=gca-gke-2025 --global 2>/dev/null || true
accelerator_availability_check.note: For DWS workloads, actual availability is determined at scheduling time by GCP — DWS will queue and retry. The pre-check is for non-DWS node pools only.

required_quota_minimums.IN_USE_ADDRESSES: Each cluster needs ~(nodes + 1) external IPs if public; 0 if private with Cloud NAT
required_quota_minimums.NVIDIA_L4_GPUS: Need exact count of GPUs requested; quota is 16 total
required_quota_minimums.NVIDIA_A100_GPUS: Quota is 16 total — use DWS for these

pre_deployment_checks.script_template: Include a pre_check.sh in verification_plan.md that runs the quota checks above before any terraform apply or kubectl apply.

cluster_type_guidance: Use GKE Autopilot for templates where the workload pattern is the focus (web apps, microservices, ML inference). Autopilot handles node management and is more cost-efficient for bursty validation workloads.
Use GKE Standard when the template requires specific node configuration: custom taints, DaemonSets, privileged containers, GPUs with specific driver config, or security scanning tools.
Always use VPC-native clusters (networking_mode = VPC_NATIVE with ip_allocation_policy block). Legacy routes-based networking is deprecated.
gke_patterns.workload_identity: Always configure Workload Identity on clusters and demonstrate the KSA→GSA binding pattern in the template. This is the canonical way for pods to access GCP APIs — never mount service account keys.

release_channels.RAPID: AI/ML templates, templates needing latest GKE features

guidance: Pin chart versions explicitly — never use 'latest' or '*' as a chart version.
Use helm_release Terraform resource for Helm deployments within the terraform-helm/ path.
Include a values.yaml file alongside the Terraform so the chart configuration is visible and reviewable.
After helm install, verify pods are Running with a null_resource / local-exec terraform block or document the verification command in verification_plan.md.

crd_placement_rule.rule: CRDs in Helm charts MUST go in the chart's crds/ subdirectory, NOT in templates/.
crd_placement_rule.why: CRDs in templates/ are tracked with Helm ownership annotations. If GKE has already installed the same CRD natively (e.g. secret_manager_config installs the Secrets Store CSI driver CRD), Helm will fail with 'cannot be imported into the current release: invalid ownership metadata'. CRDs in crds/ are installed idempotently — Helm skips them silently if they already exist.
crd_placement_rule.gke_native_crds: Enabling secret_manager_config { enabled = true } on a GKE cluster automatically installs secretproviderclasses.secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io. Do NOT include this CRD in templates/ of any Helm chart deployed to such a cluster.
crd_placement_rule.crds_dir_note: Files in crds/ are raw YAML, not Go templates — do not use {{ }} template syntax in crds/ files.

security_posture_config.rule: All GKE clusters must include a security_posture_config block (Terraform) or securityPostureConfig (KCC).

agent_metrics_reporting.description: At the end of your work on an issue (just before opening the PR), write a .agent-metrics file to the template root directory. The CI reads this to populate the Validation Record in README.md. If you cannot determine exact token counts, omit the file — the CI will record 'not recorded'.

format.input_tokens: total input tokens consumed across the entire session for this issue
format.output_tokens: total output tokens generated across the entire session for this issue
format.estimated_cost_usd: calculated cost based on the model's published pricing, formatted as a decimal e.g. 0.043
format.session_start: ISO8601 timestamp when you started working on the issue

issue_communication_protocol.description: The agent MUST post progress comments on the GitHub issue throughout its work. Do not go silent. This lets the human reviewer follow along and catch problems early.
required_checkpoints: On start: comment 'Starting work on this issue. Will generate both Terraform/Helm and Config Connector paths. Working branch: <branch-name>'
After design decision: briefly describe the architecture chosen and which reference repos you drew from
On PR creation: comment on the issue linking to the PR and summarising what was generated
On validation pass: comment 'CI passed — both TF and KCC paths deployed and torn down successfully'
On validation failure: comment with the specific error and your diagnosis before attempting a fix

reference_repositories.description: These are the canonical upstream sources. When generating any template, you MUST browse the relevant repos below to find real, working examples before writing code. Do not rely solely on training knowledge — fetch actual files from these repos to use as starting points.
reference_repositories.how_to_use: Use `gh` CLI or `curl` to fetch specific files/directories from these repos. For example: `gh api repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-foundation-toolkit/contents/infra/blueprint-test` or `curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/<org>/<repo>/main/<path>`. Always check the repo for the most current version of any module or manifest.

sources.name: Cloud Foundation Toolkit
sources.use_for: Battle-tested Terraform modules for GCP foundations (VPC, IAM, GKE). Prefer CFT modules over hand-rolled resources where available.

sources.name: Cluster Toolkit
sources.use_for: HPC and AI/ML cluster blueprints on GKE. Use for GPU/TPU node pool configs and high-performance workload patterns.

sources.name: Kubernetes Engine Samples
sources.use_for: Reference workload manifests (Deployments, Services, Ingress). Use for hello-world and real-workload Kubernetes YAML examples.

sources.name: Terraform GKE Modules
sources.use_for: The standard Terraform module for GKE clusters. Use the beta-public-cluster, private-cluster, or autopilot-cluster submodules as appropriate. Always check the examples/ directory.

sources.name: Terraform Docs Samples (GKE)
sources.use_for: Minimal, focused Terraform snippets for specific GKE features. Good for finding the exact resource block for a specific capability.

sources.name: GKE AI Labs Code Samples
sources.use_for: Concrete code for AI workloads on GKE: vLLM, Triton, JupyterHub, Ray. Fetch manifests and Terraform from here for AI/ML issues.

sources.name: LLM-D
sources.use_for: Distributed LLM inference on Kubernetes. Use for issues requiring multi-node model serving.

sources.name: Accelerated Platforms
sources.use_for: End-to-end GPU/TPU platform blueprints. Use for issues requiring accelerator-optimised clusters.

sources.name: Generative AI
sources.use_for: Vertex AI and Gemini API integration examples. Use for issues that combine GKE workloads with GCP AI services.

sources.name: GKE Policy Automation
sources.use_for: Policy and security scanning for GKE clusters. Reference for security hardening requirements in generated templates.

sources.name: Gemini for Kubernetes Development
sources.use_for: The agent infrastructure underpinning this repo. Reference for understanding the repo-agent/overseer system you are running within.

readme_specification.description: Every template directory MUST contain a README.md generated by the agent. The CI will automatically append a Validation Record section after a successful deployment. The agent is responsible for the descriptive content; the CI owns the validation data.
agent_generated_sections: ## Overview — 2-3 sentence description of the architecture and its intended use case
## Template Paths — one subsection each for terraform-helm/ and config-connector/
## Cluster Details — type (Autopilot/Standard), release channel, node pools, machine types, networking mode
## Workload Details — what application is deployed, how to access it (endpoint type, port, auth)
## Enabled Features — checklist of GKE/GCP features used (see required_features_checklist)
required_features_checklist: Config Connector managed resources (list each GCP resource type)
GPU node pool with driver auto-install (if applicable)
readme_specification.ci_appended_section: The CI appends a '## Validation Record' table at the bottom of README.md after successful deployment. Do not write this section — CI owns it. It includes: status icons, date, deployment duration, region, zones, cluster name, and commit SHA for both the TF+Helm and KCC paths.
readme_specification.example_readme_structure:
# Template: Private GKE + Cloud SQL

## Overview
Deploys a private GKE Standard cluster with a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance managed via Config Connector. Workloads connect to the database using Cloud SQL Auth Proxy and Workload Identity.

## Template Paths
### Terraform + Helm (`terraform-helm/`)
- Provisions VPC, private GKE cluster, Cloud NAT, and Cloud SQL via Terraform
- Deploys a sample app via Helm with the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar

### Config Connector (`config-connector/`)
- Manages SQLInstance, SQLDatabase, SQLUser, and IAMPolicyMember as KCC resources
- Cluster provisioned separately via Terraform

## Cluster Details
- **Type**: GKE Standard, zonal (us-central1-b)
- **Release channel**: REGULAR
- **Node pool**: e2-standard-4, spot, autoscaling 1–3
- **Networking**: VPC-native, private nodes, Cloud NAT

## Workload Details
- **Application**: Sample Python app (Flask)
- **Access**: Internal LoadBalancer on port 8080
- **Database**: PostgreSQL 15 via Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar

## Enabled Features
- [x] Workload Identity
- [x] VPC-native networking
- [x] Private cluster with Cloud NAT
- [x] Config Connector — SQLInstance, SQLDatabase, IAMPolicyMember
- [x] Horizontal Pod Autoscaler

guidelines: For every issue, start by identifying which reference_repositories are most relevant to the intent, then FETCH actual files from those repos before writing any code.
Support dual-path IaC generation: Terraform/Helm and Config Connector.

For every issue requiring infrastructure generation, the agent MUST create two distinct folders under templates/<short-name>/: one named config-connector/ for Config Connector manifests, and one named terraform-helm/ for Terraform/Helm files. The directory name is the template identity — do NOT prefix with an issue number.
All generated cloud resources (clusters, networks, buckets) MUST be named after the template (e.g., enterprise-gke-cluster, enterprise-gke-vpc) to ensure uniqueness and easy cleanup.

Never use hardcoded project IDs. Use the project_id from project_context above (gca-gke-2025). For Terraform: var.project_id defaulting to 'gca-gke-2025'. For KCC: annotate with cnrm.cloud.google.com/project-id.
Every template must include a verification_plan.md with exact commands to deploy, verify, and destroy both the KCC and TF/Helm paths.
Follow the issue_communication_protocol — post comments at each checkpoint.
Ensure templates are security scanned and work out-of-the-box.
Consider and factor in new GKE features if the example architectures could benefit from them.
When selecting GKE release channels: Use RAPID for templates requiring bleeding-edge features or AI/ML workloads. Use REGULAR or STABLE for standard enterprise templates.

sandbox_constraints.description: All resources created during sandbox validation MUST be fully deleteable by automation without manual intervention. Violating these constraints will cause terraform destroy and kubectl delete to fail, leaving orphaned resources and incurring ongoing costs.
gke_cluster_rules: MANDATORY: Every google_container_cluster resource MUST include deletion_protection = false. The Terraform provider defaults to true, which will prevent automated teardown.
MANDATORY: Output cluster_name and cluster_location from every terraform-helm/main.tf so the CI can verify the cluster is healthy before teardown.
RECOMMENDED: Use spot = true or preemptible = true in node_config to minimise sandbox cost.
kcc_rules: MANDATORY: Do NOT add the annotation cnrm.cloud.google.com/deletion-policy: abandon to any KCC resource.
MANDATORY: Apply all KCC resources with the label template: <template-dir-name> so the CI can target them precisely.
MANDATORY: Do NOT set spec.deletionPolicy: Retain on any KCC resource.
RECOMMENDED: Set spec.resourceID explicitly using the template name as a suffix (e.g., enterprise-gke-cluster).
terraform_backend: MANDATORY: Every terraform-helm/ directory must include an empty backend 'gcs' block — the CI injects bucket and prefix at runtime. Do not hardcode these values.
MANDATORY: Include a versions.tf that pins the google and google-beta provider versions to avoid drift: ~> 6.0

success_criteria.definition: A successful issue resolution requires a live interaction with a running workload endpoint — not just a healthy cluster. Both the Terraform+Helm and KCC paths must demonstrate this independently.

endpoint_interaction_requirement.description: The agent MUST interact with the deployed workload's endpoint and capture proof of a successful response before declaring success. A cluster in RUNNING state is NOT sufficient on its own.
endpoint_interaction_requirement.for_public_endpoints: curl or wget the external IP/hostname and assert a non-5xx HTTP response. Capture the status code and a snippet of the response body.
endpoint_interaction_requirement.for_private_endpoints: kubectl exec into a test pod on the cluster and run curl from inside the cluster. Document the internal endpoint and the response.
endpoint_interaction_requirement.for_non_http_workloads: Demonstrate functional interaction appropriate to the workload type: psql query for databases, gcloud pubsub subscriptions pull for Pub/Sub, kubectl exec nvidia-smi for GPU workloads.
endpoint_interaction_requirement.proof_format: Append the endpoint interaction output to verification_plan.md under a '## Validation Output' section, including the command run and the response received.

requirements: Cluster and workloads are deployed and all pods are Running/Ready.
MANDATORY: A live interaction with the workload endpoint succeeds and produces a valid response (see endpoint_interaction_requirement).
Every PR generated by the agent MUST include a verification_plan.md with exact deploy, verify, and destroy commands for both paths.
verification_plan.md MUST include the actual endpoint URL or internal address used during validation.
Terraform destroy completes without error (requires deletion_protection = false on all clusters).
KCC resource deletion (kubectl delete) results in the GCP resource being removed — verify with gcloud.
success_criteria.failure_handling: If deployments fail, look at failures, research options, and work to resolve. Always look back at the ask in the original issue. Post a comment on the issue explaining the failure and your intended fix. Do not mark an issue resolved without a successful endpoint interaction.
